ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for digestive biscuit with fruit or nut; 'biscuit food product' (FOODON_03301710) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 category A00LC (Biscuits and crackers) is the closest defensible match; no specific code exists for digestive biscuit with fruit or nut variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for digestive biscuit with fruit or nut; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is 2 digestive biscuits (approximately 30 g), commonly consumed as a snack or with tea.

nutrient_source.title: Digestive Biscuit (Fruit or Nut) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Digestive biscuit (fruit or nut), CR ID F11080022, confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at 9.5 g/100g based on typical palm-oil-containing digestive biscuit composition cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for digestive biscuits (FDC ID 172023); monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and other micronutrients set to -1 where not reported by HPB. Calcium reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB; confirmed zero per source.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 70 for digestive biscuits is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table (Diabetes Care), which lists plain digestive biscuits at GI ~59–70 depending on formulation. The fruit-or-nut variant with higher sugar content (23.6 g/100g) is assigned GI 70 (high end of the published range) as the dried fruit raises the effective glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (70 × 63.23 × 0.30) / 100 = 13.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (23.6 g/100g) and elevated sodium (283 mg/100g) with high saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g from palm oil)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this biscuit: the very high sugar content (23.6 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and raises blood pressure, while sodium at 283 mg/100g and saturated fat from palm oil (~9.5 g/100g) further compound cardiovascular risk; limit to an occasional small portion and prefer lower-sugar, lower-sodium wholegrain alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar (23.6 g/100g) exceeding the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling, high GI (70), and high carbohydrate load (63.2 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (2.85 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this biscuit: sugar at 23.6 g/100g exceeds the 20 g/100g hard gate, the high GI of 70 causes rapid postprandial glucose spikes, and the low fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio (2.85 g fibre per 63.2 g carbohydrate) provides minimal glycaemic buffering; if a biscuit is desired, choose a plain wholemeal digestive with sugar below 10 g/100g and consume no more than one biscuit per sitting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g from palm oil), trans fat (~0.1 g/100g), high sugar (23.6 g/100g), and elevated sodium (283 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should strictly limit consumption of this biscuit: saturated fat at approximately 9.5 g/100g (predominantly from palm oil) raises LDL cholesterol, trans fat at ~0.1 g/100g is pro-atherogenic at any amount, high sugar promotes triglyceride synthesis, and sodium at 283 mg/100g contributes to blood pressure elevation; this product is not recommended as a regular snack for those managing CVD risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (~0.1 g/100g), high saturated fat from palm oil (~9.5 g/100g), and high sugar (23.6 g/100g) are all pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this biscuit: trans fat at ~0.1 g/100g is strongly pro-inflammatory and triggers the RA hard gate regardless of other factors, palm-oil-derived saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) worsens joint inflammation and is excluded from the MUFA/PUFA anti-inflammatory carve-out, and high sugar (23.6 g/100g) promotes inflammatory mediators.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Digestive Biscuit (Fruit or Nut) is a baked wholemeal wheat biscuit enriched with added dried fruits (such as raisins or sultanas) and/or nuts (such as almonds or hazelnuts), giving it a sweeter and more textured profile than a plain digestive biscuit. The biscuit has a crumbly, semi-crisp texture with a mildly sweet, nutty, and slightly caramel-like flavour derived from the wholemeal flour, sugar, palm oil, and fruit inclusions. It is typically consumed as a snack, often paired with tea or coffee, and is widely available in Singapore supermarkets as an imported or locally packaged product. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 485 kcal/100g, with high fat (22.1 g/100g, predominantly saturated fat ~9.5 g/100g from palm oil), very high sugar (23.6 g/100g from added sugar and concentrated dried fruit), and moderate protein (7.75 g/100g). Dietary fibre is low at 2.85 g/100g relative to the carbohydrate load of 63.2 g/100g. Sodium is elevated at 283 mg/100g. The glycemic index is high (GI 70), making this biscuit a rapid glucose-raising food. Clinically, this product is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its combination of high sugar, high saturated fat, trans fat, and elevated sodium. It is plant-based, wholegrain-based, and dairy-free, but contains gluten and tree nuts as allergens. It is not recommended as a regular snack for health-conscious consumers or those with chronic disease.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11080022, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cross-reference for palm-oil-containing digestive biscuits. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed international GI table. All values are internally consistent and plausible for this food category.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific fruit-or-nut digestive biscuit variant are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11080022) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F11080022). trans_fat_g is estimated at 0.1 g/100g based on typical palm-oil-containing biscuit formulation; this value is not explicitly reported by HPB but is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for comparable products. saturated_fat_g estimated at 9.5 g/100g from USDA FoodData Central cross-reference (FDC ID 172023, digestive biscuits) given that HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. GI of 70 assigned at the high end of the published range (59–70) for digestive biscuits per University of Sydney GI Database, reflecting the elevated sugar content from dried fruit inclusions. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as (70 × 63.23 × 0.30) / 100 = 13.3 g. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. The food is of British origin and is consumed globally including Singapore as an imported packaged product.
